section.title: The Oracle against Tyre
section.sectionRationale: This closes the long gallery of oracles against the nations, chapters 13 to 23, with Tyre, the great Phoenician trading power whose wealth and pride made her the merchant of the nations. Her fall humbles maritime commerce itself, yet the oracle ends with a startling promise: after seventy years, Tyre's profits will be consecrated to the LORD rather than merely hoarded.
section.thesis: The LORD who planned Tyre's humbling, to strip the pride of a city that made merchandise its glory, will also, after seventy years, turn that same wealth toward His own purposes, showing that even commerce and trade can finally be brought under His lordship.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the wailing ships and the news of Tyre's ruin; the LORD's deliberate plan to disgrace the renowned of the earth; a glance at Chaldea's own earlier ruin at Assyria's hand; and the promise that after seventy years Tyre's restored profit will be set apart to the LORD.

units.label: Wail, O ships of Tarshish
units.note: the merchant of the nations laid waste

verses.text: This is the burden against Tyre: Wail, O ships of Tarshish, for Tyre is laid waste, without house or harbor. Word has reached them from the land of Cyprus.
Be silent, O dwellers of the coastland, you merchants of Sidon, whose traders have crossed the sea.
On the great waters came the grain of Shihor; the harvest of the Nile was the revenue of Tyre; she was the merchant of the nations.
Be ashamed, O Sidon, the stronghold of the sea, for the sea has spoken: “I have not been in labor or given birth. I have not raised young men or brought up young women.”
When the report reaches Egypt, they will writhe in agony over the news of Tyre.

units.label: Who planned this against Tyre? The LORD planned it

verses.text: Cross over to Tarshish; wail, O inhabitants of the coastland!
Is this your jubilant city, whose origin is from antiquity, whose feet have taken her to settle far away?
Who planned this against Tyre, the bestower of crowns, whose traders are princes, whose merchants are renowned on the earth?
The LORD of Hosts planned it, to defile all its glorious beauty, to disgrace all the renowned of the earth.
Cultivate your land like the Nile, O Daughter of Tarshish; there is no longer a harbor.
The LORD has stretched out His hand over the sea; He has made kingdoms tremble. He has given a command that the strongholds of Canaan be destroyed.
He said, “You shall rejoice no more, O oppressed Virgin Daughter of Sidon. Get up and cross over to Cyprus, even there you will find no rest.”

units.label: Look at the Chaldeans, ruined before her

verses.text: Look at the land of the Chaldeans, a people now of no account. The Assyrians destined it for the desert creatures; they set up their siege towers and stripped its palaces. They brought it to ruin.
Wail, O ships of Tarshish, for your harbor has been destroyed!

units.label: Seventy years, then restored, her profit set apart to the LORD

verses.text: At that time Tyre will be forgotten for seventy years, the span of a king's life. But at the end of seventy years, it will happen to Tyre as in the song of the harlot:
“Take up your harp, stroll through the city, O forgotten harlot. Make sweet melody, sing many a song, so you will be remembered.”
And at the end of seventy years, the LORD will restore Tyre. Then she will return to hire as a {{zanah|prostitute}} and sell herself to all the kingdoms on the face of the earth.
Yet her {{sachar|profits}} and wages will be set apart to the LORD; they will not be stored or saved, for her profit will go to those who live before the LORD, for abundant food and fine clothing.

sachar.gloss: Trade, merchandise, or the profit gained through commerce. Tyre's whole identity was built on sachar; the oracle's final surprise is that this same profit, once devoted only to accumulating wealth, will one day be set apart to the LORD instead.

zanah.gloss: To act as a prostitute, or by extension to sell oneself indiscriminately for gain. Tyre's aggressive, morally indiscriminate pursuit of trade with every nation is pictured as prostitution, yet even this profit, the oracle says, will eventually be consecrated to God rather than merely hoarded.

basic.context: Tyre was the great Phoenician trading city whose ships and merchants dominated Mediterranean commerce, so wealthy and renowned that her traders were called princes. This oracle announces her sudden humbling, sending shockwaves as far as Egypt and the Nile delta that depended on her trade, and closes the long collection of oracles against the nations that began in chapter 13.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes a real, historically anchored judgment on Tyre's pride in her wealth and trading empire, likely fulfilled in stages, including a thirteen year siege by Nebuchadnezzar and later conquest by Alexander the Great. The seventy years and Tyre's eventual restored trade being set apart to the LORD, verses 15 to 18, describes an actual, if partial, historical renewal of Tyre's commerce rather than a purely symbolic promise, while also anticipating the wider biblical hope of the nations' wealth serving God's purposes.
basic.originalLanguages: Tyre's entire identity rests on sachar, trade and profit, the word used throughout this oracle for her commercial glory. Her relentless pursuit of profit with every nation is described using the verb zanah, to prostitute oneself, yet astonishingly the oracle ends by saying this same profit will finally be set apart to the LORD rather than merely accumulated for herself.
basic.typology: Tyre's wealth finally being consecrated to the LORD rather than hoarded anticipates the New Testament's vision of the nations' glory and wealth being brought into the New Jerusalem, Revelation 21:24-26, commerce and craftsmanship redeemed for God's purposes rather than mere self-enrichment.
basic.christ: Tyre's aggressive, morally indiscriminate pursuit of wealth is judged, yet even her profit is finally destined to serve the LORD's people, a small preview of the greater gospel truth that Christ redeems and redirects even what was once given to selfish or corrupt purposes, so that the wealth of the nations ultimately serves and glorifies God, Revelation 21:24-26.
basic.god: God is sovereign over international trade and commerce, not merely armies and kings; He deliberately planned Tyre's humbling to disgrace pride that exalted itself as the renowned merchant of the nations. Yet His purposes do not end in mere destruction; He intends, after judgment, to draw even worldly wealth into service of His own people and worship.
basic.crossReferences: Ezekiel 26 to 28 gives an extended companion oracle against Tyre, detailing her pride and fall in much greater depth. Matthew 15:21-28 shows Jesus ministering near Tyre and Sidon, extending grace to a woman from this very region. Revelation 18 echoes Tyre's merchant imagery in describing Babylon's commercial fall. Revelation 21:24-26 pictures the nations' wealth and glory finally brought into the New Jerusalem.
basic.oneStory: Tyre's proud, self-serving wealth is humbled by the LORD's design, yet even this judgment is not the last word; her restored profit is destined to be set apart for God's people. This anticipates the larger biblical hope that the wealth and skill of every nation, redeemed rather than destroyed, will finally serve and glorify God through Christ.

context.literary: The final and, in some ways, most ironic oracle in the collection of chapters 13 to 23, built around repeated cries to wail addressed to the ships of Tarshish, framing Tyre's fall as news that ripples outward across the whole trading world she once dominated, before an unexpected coda of restoration and consecration.
context.historical: Tyre, built partly on a fortified island, was famed for maritime trade reaching Tarshish, likely in Spain, and Cyprus; she suffered a thirteen year siege under Nebuchadnezzar in the sixth century BC and was finally taken by Alexander the Great in 332 BC after he built a causeway to her island stronghold. The mention of seventy years likely parallels the length of Judah's own Babylonian exile, suggesting a period of eclipse followed by a real historical recovery of Tyre's trade.
context.canonical: Closes the sequence of oracles against the nations begun in Isaiah 13, and finds an extended companion treatment in Ezekiel 26 to 28, which develops Tyre's pride, described there under the image of the king of Tyre, into one of Scripture's richest pictures of the fall of arrogant human glory.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To announce that the LORD, not merely regional politics, humbles the pride of the world's great trading power, and to promise that even Tyre's wealth, purified through judgment, will ultimately serve His purposes.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific historical judgment and eventual partial restoration of Tyre's commerce; it does not prescribe a specific economic policy. Its abiding principle is that wealth and trade, however impressive, remain under God's sovereignty and are ultimately meant to serve His purposes rather than mere self-glorification.

debates.question: How should Tyre being compared to a prostitute who returns to her trade, verses 16 to 17, be understood alongside the promise that her profit will be set apart to the LORD, verse 18?

views.label: The imagery highlights continuity of Tyre's morally compromised methods even after restoration
views.case: Tyre's commercial dealings, described as prostitution because she sold herself indiscriminately to every nation for profit, would resume in largely the same form after her seventy years, showing that God's plan does not require every human institution to be perfected before He puts it to use.

views.label: The imagery contrasts Tyre's old self-serving trade with a new, God-directed purpose for her wealth
views.case: Verse 18 marks a genuine change: profit once hoarded for herself will now go to those who live before the LORD, suggesting the restoration includes a real redirection of Tyre's wealth toward God's people, even if her trading methods remain recognizably the same.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms that God can take wealth generated by flawed or even morally compromised means and direct it toward His own purposes, a pattern of redemptive repurposing rather than requiring every economic system to be perfected before it can serve Him.

study.originalLanguages: Tyre's whole identity is bound up in sachar, trade and profit, repeated throughout the oracle as the source of her pride and eventual humbling. Her relentless, indiscriminate pursuit of that profit is likened to zanah, prostitution, yet the oracle's final surprise is that this same profit will be set apart to the LORD, a redemptive turn embedded within a judgment oracle.

typology.type: Tyre's wealth finally set apart to the LORD rather than hoarded
typology.fulfillment: The nations bringing their glory and wealth into the New Jerusalem, Revelation 21:24-26, commerce and craftsmanship redeemed for God's purposes.

study.christ: Tyre's proud, self-serving commerce is judged, yet the oracle's unexpected ending, her profit finally set apart to the LORD, anticipates the wider gospel pattern of redemption: Christ does not merely destroy what sin has corrupted but redirects it toward God's glory. Centuries later, Jesus Himself ministered near Tyre and Sidon, extending faith-honoring grace to a woman from that very region, Matthew 15:21-28, a living picture of this very oracle's hope that even Tyre's people would come to know and serve the LORD.
study.god: God's sovereignty reaches into international trade and commerce, not merely armies and thrones; He deliberately planned to humble Tyre's pride in her own wealth and renown. Yet His purposes for the nations are ultimately redemptive, not merely punitive; He intends even Tyre's restored wealth to be set apart for His people's good, showing that no earthly resource lies outside His plan to redeem it for His own purposes.

crossReferences.note: An extended companion oracle detailing Tyre's pride and fall, including the famous lament over the king of Tyre.
Jesus ministers with grace to a woman from the region of Tyre and Sidon, a living picture of this oracle's hope that Tyre's people would come to know the LORD.
Echoes Tyre's merchant imagery in describing the fall of Babylon's worldwide commercial system.
Pictures the wealth and glory of the nations finally brought into the New Jerusalem, the ultimate fulfillment of Tyre's profit being set apart to the LORD.

study.oneStory: Tyre's proud, self-serving wealth is humbled by the LORD's own design, yet even this judgment ends in an unexpected hope: her restored profit will be set apart for God's people rather than merely hoarded. This oracle anticipates the whole biblical hope that the wealth, skill, and commerce of every nation, redeemed rather than merely destroyed, will finally serve and glorify God through Christ.

quiz.q: What made Tyre so renowned before her judgment in this oracle?

options.text: Her vast maritime trade, with merchants called princes, renowned across the earth.
options.note: Verses 3 and 8 describe exactly this trading glory and renown.

options.text: Her powerful standing army and land conquests.
options.note: The oracle focuses on Tyre's commercial, maritime glory, not land-based military conquest.

options.text: Her role as a center of pagan religious pilgrimage.
options.note: Not the focus of this oracle; her fame here is specifically commercial.

options.text: Her agricultural exports of grain and wine.
options.note: Grain and produce from the Nile passed through her, verse 3, but her core identity was trade itself, not her own agriculture.

quiz.q: Who does the oracle say planned Tyre's humbling, and why?

options.text: The LORD of Hosts planned it, to disgrace all the renowned of the earth.
options.note: Verse 9 states this explicitly: the LORD planned it to humble pride.

options.text: Rival merchants from Sidon planned it out of jealousy.
options.note: The text attributes the plan to the LORD, not commercial rivals.

options.text: It happened purely by economic accident with no divine involvement.
options.note: The oracle explicitly credits the LORD's deliberate plan, not chance.

options.text: The Assyrians alone planned and carried it out.
options.note: Human armies act as instruments, but the text credits the ultimate planning to the LORD Himself.

quiz.q: What historical precedent does verse 13 point to?

options.text: The earlier ruin of the Chaldeans at the hands of the Assyrians.
options.note: Verse 13 explicitly recalls the Chaldeans being reduced to ruin by Assyria as a precedent.

options.text: The destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah.
options.note: Not mentioned in this passage; the precedent given is the Chaldeans and Assyria.

options.text: The exodus from Egypt.
options.note: Not referenced in this specific verse.

options.text: The building of the first temple.
options.note: Unrelated to this verse's actual content, which recalls a nation's military ruin.

quiz.q: What happens to Tyre's profit after her seventy years of being forgotten, according to verse 18?

options.text: It will be set apart to the LORD, going to those who live before Him.
options.note: Verse 18 states this surprising redirection of Tyre's restored wealth explicitly.

options.text: It will be permanently destroyed and never recovered.
options.note: The opposite; her trade is restored and her profit redirected, not destroyed.

options.text: It will be seized entirely by the king of Babylon.
options.note: Not stated in this passage; the profit is directed toward the LORD's people, not a foreign king.

options.text: It will be equally divided among all the nations she once traded with.
options.note: The text specifies the profit serves those who live before the LORD, not an equal distribution among all nations.

quiz.q: How does Tyre's restored, consecrated wealth anticipate the New Testament?

options.text: It anticipates the nations' wealth and glory being brought into the New Jerusalem.
options.note: Revelation 21:24-26 pictures exactly this final ingathering of the nations' wealth to God's glory.

options.text: It shows that all wealth is inherently evil and must be destroyed.
options.note: The opposite; this oracle envisions wealth redeemed and redirected, not simply destroyed.

options.text: It has no connection to any New Testament theme.
options.note: There is a clear thematic link to Revelation's vision of the nations' wealth serving God's glory.

options.text: It refers only to Tyre's literal ancient economy with no further significance.
options.note: The historical fulfillment is real, but Scripture also draws forward theological significance from this pattern of redeemed wealth.

questions: Tyre's identity and pride were built entirely on her wealth and trade. What achievement, possession, or reputation do you build your own identity on more than you should?
The LORD deliberately planned to disgrace all the renowned of the earth. Where do you see pride, your own or a system you are part of, that God may be working to humble?

Tyre's profit, once pursued indiscriminately, was finally set apart to the LORD. What resources, skills, or income do you have that could be more deliberately dedicated to God's purposes rather than only your own?
Jesus later ministered with grace to a woman from Tyre and Sidon's region. Who in your life feels as far outside God's reach as ancient Tyre, and how might God be inviting you to extend grace there?
This oracle shows that even judged, compromised systems can be redeemed for God's purposes. Where do you need hope that a broken situation in your own life is not beyond God's redemptive power?

translationNotes.note: Tarshish likely refers to a distant western trading port, possibly in Spain, representing the far reach of Tyre's maritime trade network.
Where BSB prints an em dash before even there you will find no rest, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
Where BSB prints an em dash after Chaldeans, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
Where BSB prints an em dash before the span of a king's life, it has been rendered as a comma per house style; seventy years likely parallels the length of Judah's own Babylonian exile.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 23:1-18. Poetic line breaks joined into running verse text; where BSB prints an em dash it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
